questions.question: Why are map-tile systems often dominated by cache strategy more than write throughput?
options: Because reads dominate heavily, so immutability and cache layering shape latency and cost the most.
Because map data never changes after the first publish.
Because tiles are too small to benefit from CDNs.
Because publish paths must stay synchronous with every user request.
questions.explanation: Read-mostly systems win primarily on cacheability and rollout discipline.

questions.question: What is the main benefit of immutable versioned tile paths?
options: They allow long-lived caches and safer rollback without global purge dependence.
They eliminate the need for any tile metadata API.
They make origin storage unnecessary after the first request.
They guarantee every region flips versions simultaneously.
questions.explanation: Versioned paths make cached data safe to keep around.

questions.question: Why should the active tile version usually be controlled by a manifest or metadata layer?
options: It lets clients switch versions cheaply without mutating or purging all cached tile URLs.
It ensures browser caches are disabled during publish windows.
It prevents any need for canary rollout by construction.
It replaces the need to validate tile checksums.
questions.explanation: A small control-plane decision is easier to flip than the whole asset corpus.

questions.question: Which metric best reveals that one zoom level is creating origin pressure?
options: Edge hit rate and origin offload by zoom level, especially on hot metropolitan regions.
The count of map designers reviewing tile style mockups.
The number of tile schema comments left in pull requests.
The age of the oldest archived tile version in cold storage.
questions.explanation: Zoom-specific miss patterns show where the cache plan is weak.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with keeping multiple active tile versions during rollout?
options: It makes rollback easier but consumes more storage and operational tracking.
It guarantees worse cache hit rate in every case.
It removes the need for regional canaries.
It forces the browser to request every version on each pan.
questions.explanation: Extra versions buy safety at a storage and coordination cost.

questions.question: What is the most credible way to avoid a purge storm during tile publishes?
options: Publish immutable assets and flip a version reference instead of purging mutable paths everywhere.
Purge the full CDN globally before every regional update.
Set all TTLs to zero so clients always revalidate.
Move all tile requests directly to origin during publish windows.
questions.explanation: Manifest-based rollout is the usual escape from expensive broad invalidation.

questions.question: Why is targeted prewarm usually stronger than prewarming the whole world?
options: Traffic is concentrated in certain zooms and regions, so full prewarm wastes cost and time.
Cold tiles can never be cached by design.
Prewarm only matters for vector tiles, never raster.
It guarantees no cache misses for long-tail regions.
questions.explanation: Most value comes from warming the hot path, not every possible tile.

questions.question: Which redesign is most likely if one subset of map layers must update every few minutes?
options: Separate faster-changing layers or manifests so they can roll independently from slower base tiles.
Collapse every layer back into one mutable URL per tile.
Disable CDN caching for all layers globally.
Remove version tracking and let clients guess freshness.
questions.explanation: Different update cadences usually deserve different rollout units.
